replies.text:
He _empowers_ you to DIY, so try out. He will not take that burden from you. 
He will come back to the material, if he is allowed. 
Just use some old heating wire spiral and wind it around your sugar bowl, need a temperature sensor, and wrap around it some chimney Rockwool insulation that is temperature resistant (F90). Use outsides on non-flammable ground, sand… 

I already bought a fine scale 1/100 Gramms resolution. So I can weigh the hydrogen uptake. 


I will use a heliostat setup, and include some small double-glass quartz window DIY in the insulation to shoot in concentrated solar light. Just blacken some outer ceramics pot with soot.
I will steer temperature by adjusting focus and tracking of the mirror, and use electricity only when a cloud shades the sun.

Perhaps, in future, I will try out add-on microwave heating able to penetrate the inner insulation (for glass melting, this works exceptionally well)

text:
Problem Virus -> solution: antiviral:
Can you use graphite plates to electrolyse purified salt in purified distilled water to gain HCIO solution 800ppm for disinfection purposes? How do I prevent contact of the first metal plate to the salt water?
On ebay, one can buy ruthetium-iridium-titanium - electrode plate staples, claiming to be more efficient. 
If it makes a cleaner solution, very well invested 50€. But I feel for 800ppm the demand is not as high. 

Or some salt pool Chlorinator. Probably useable for a whole refugee camp or clinic or city. 


If you want to know why it is so interesting, imagine you can inhale it totally tolerable, we did it in 20 families, among other effective, safe, cheap and available agents.
Provided it is pure and not higher than 800ppm (measure by test stripe reading ppm HCIO or thin to measuring range of Cl pool tester: 375ppm Cl equals 800ppm NaHCIO) and not more than 0.6ml per session, for standard lung surface (lung fluid volume diluting the aerosol) of a 70kg person. For eg 10yr old we just used 0.3 to 0.4 per session. Let run inhaler only while breathing in or count breathing in time. As it is self-disinfecting, we did a circle when many family members had CoV, inhaling slowly just the nebula jet without touching the outlet.
See
Inhalation of a fog of hypochlorous acid : Biochemical, antimicrobial, and pathological assessment

author: @PeterPete
text: We've determined a great cheap material to store hydrogen and it's everywhere!! Yes, water is the best cheap hydrogen storage solution. We placed hydrogen gas in a small glass bottle and placed it inside a large glass coffee jar filled to the brim with water. We capped it off and then placed the jar submerged in a bucket of water and left it there for approx 9 months. After this time, we found there was no loss of the hydrogen gas in the small glass bottle. In our view the reason why water stores hydrogen really well is because not only is there no hydrogen contained in water but also because hydrogen is immiscible in water.

Quick math: 
1 kg of hydrogen contains 33.33 kWh of usable energy
Tesla Model 3 (Std Range Plus?)

Consumption Rate: 15 kWh/100 km
Typical Range: 360 km (225 miles)
Energy comparable H2 mass: 1.62 Kg
Required gCN to store H2@10%wt: 16.2 Kg
gCN cost: $324,000  (@$20,000 per Kg)
kWh capacity cost:$6,000/kWh  ($324,000/54)

author: @aomanchutube
text:
So if gasoline is approximately 2.8kg/gallon and my Prius C has an 11.9gal tank = 33kg and if 1kg of hydrogen has the same energy as 2.8kg of gasoline, then we would need around 11kg of hydrogen. But you can only store it at 10% weight to the carbon storage canister... ignoring the sheet metal tank it will be kept in..we would need a 110kg tank. 

So is it possible to store more than 10% by adding pressure??? 

But wow, this is great for storing hydrogen at home as energy storage for sure! For example here in Washington state, one could have a wind turbine running an electrolysis machine and storing the hydrogen for usage later that night or maybe for use during a blackout or for winter heating.

author: @Frossglass
text: Hey I have an idea of a method to produce sulfuric acid either by pyrolisys or decomposition of organic material. If you get the maximum amount of sulfhidric acid and the minimum of CO2 and methane, then with something similar to Claus process you get elementary sulfur (https://drum.lib.umd.edu/handle/1903/13531). Then you burn elementarý sulfur to get sulfur dioxide, and pass it through a gas reactor to react with ozone, I have searched a lot but I am not sure If this works better in presence of water, at high temperatures, pressures, catalyst, etc. So you should get sulfur trioxide which reacts with water to form sulfuric acid, and I think that some porous material is neded in this reaction in order to get the maximum yield (in case of bubbling SO3 in liquid water, forming very small bubbles), or in other case, reacting SO3 with water vapor in a gas reactor. Now for the sulfur source, I know that keratin has a lot of sulfur in it, however hair is difficult to be decompossed by bacteria, and think that pyrolisys is the way to go (yes I'm crazy and thinking about when get my haircut, save the hair and burn it to get sulfuric acid), however I don't know other high sulfur content sources.

author: @PressToDigitate
text: IF the IgCN on GoreTex works - pretty much *at all* - for Proton Exchange Membrane and Gas Diffusion Electrode applications, the chance that it needs more material or a larger area/volume to perform the same electrolysis or fuel cell function as compared to Platinum on Nafion, isn't really a show stopper.  The IgCN lends itself to improvement in nanostructure with better processing, whereas Platinum is already 'all that it can be'.  The difference between Nafion and GoreTex appears to have been determined by DuPont's marketing department, rather than the scientists or engineers involved in creating the material; it seems that it was differentiated for sales purposes, more than anything else, and then somewhat incrementally enhanced for specific applications, but the two started out as one thing.  A complete system for low cost, third world Hydrogen production, storage and power generation - from Solar directly and from other renewables by electrolysis - is in the offing, if you made such a diffusion membrane work from these terribly cheap ingredients.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure mate - are you working on it?

replies.author: @PressToDigitate
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I had intended to a couple years ago, inspired by your work.  But don't currently have the setup to do experiments.  You've got everything at hand except a few square feet of GoreTex, which is quite cheap as bulk fabric, or could even be cannibalized from used sportswear.   I'm not the first to consider GoreTex vs. Nafion:  https://books.google.ee/books?id=CcjrAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA94&lpg=PA94&dq=Bulk+GoreTex&source=bl&ots=zsk5iO4p_3&sig=ACfU3U2K8BQTzh9SUDZenU2am291NCA0ag&hl=en&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjn_YioxoTqAhUCy6QKHRuNASYQ6AEwEHoECA0QAQ#v=onepage&q=Bulk%20GoreTex&f=false
